data.title: Allete: Opportunity For One Last Bite Of The Cherry
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Background

In previous articles on Allete, Inc. (NYSE:ALE), I have explained how superior returns might be achieved buying at the then current share price and waiting for the $67 payment promised on completion of the Merger first announced on May 5, 2024, per the filing ("the Merger").

Key issues have been if and when the Merger, which is subject to shareholder and various regulatory approvals, might close. The Minnesota Public Utilities Commission ("MPUC") has now agreed to the Merger of Allete, Inc. per the excerpt below from an October 3, 2025 press release by Allete:

ALLETE, Inc. (NYSE: ALE) (the Company) today announced that the Minnesota Public Utilities Commission (MPUC) voted unanimously to approve its acquisition by Canada Pension Plan Investment Board (CPP Investments) and Global Infrastructure Partners (GIP). With all required regulatory approvals secured, the transaction is expected to close in late 2025 following issuance of the MPUC’s written order.

Subsequent to the May 5, 2024 Merger announcement, on October 1, 2024, BlackRock, Inc. (NYSE: BLK) announced the successful completion of its acquisition of Global Infrastructure Partners (“GIP”).

Common Stock Shareholders' Dividend Entitlement

In regard to quarterly dividends, the Merger agreement (linked above) prohibits payments of dividends with exceptions per following excerpts:

('A') quarterly cash dividends payable by the Company in respect of shares of Company Common Stock with declaration, record, increase and payment dates consistent with the Company’s past practices in an amount per share of Company Common Stock not in excess of five percent (5%) per share more than the dividend payable during the prior 12-month period,... and ('D') a “stub period” dividend to holders of record of Company Common Stock as of immediately prior to the Effective Time equal to the product of (1) the number of days from the record date for payment of the last quarterly dividend paid by the Company prior to the Effective Time, multiplied by (2) a daily dividend rate determined by dividing the amount of the last quarterly dividend paid prior to the Effective Time by ninety-one (91).

Accordingly, a further quarterly dividend payment can be expected by common stock shareholders ("the Shareholders"), before or at the time of payment of the $67 per share Merger acquisition price. There was an annual dividend increase of $0.025 per share per quarter in the first quarter of 2025 and increases of $0.0275 in each of the previous two years. Based on past practices, shareholders should expect a declaration by Allete on October 25, 2025 of a quarterly dividend at the existing rate of $0.73 per share, with the Record Date and Ex-Div date November 15, 2025, and Pay date December 1, 2025. If the Merger is not completed until after November 15, 2025, the Shareholders will be entitled to additional dividend payment per share per day of $0.73 divided by 91.

Opportunity For One Last Bite Of The Cherry

Let's assume the Merger is completed and payment is made to the Shareholders on December 31, 2025. Existing Shareholders have various options available: sell now at or around the October 22, 2025 closing share price of $67.34; hold through completion of the Merger; buy additional shares to hold through completion. Potential new shareholders have the option to buy and hold through completion. Shareholders holding existing shares or buying shares effective October 23, 2025 and holding through December 31, 2025 could expect to receive a dividend payment of $0.73 on December 1, 2025, and a further payment of $67.37 ($67 plus stub period dividend $0.37). The expected rate of return over the period October 23, 2025 through December 31, 2025 is shown in Table 1 below. A scenario where completion occurs by December 1 is also included in Table 1.

Table 1

Table 1 indicates at a buy price of $67.34 for Allete stock, there is a relatively high degree of certainty of achieving a return between 6% and 7% between now and the end of the year. For idle cash that can be deployed for a couple of months that is probably better than leaving it in the bank.

Allete likely remains a hold for existing shareholdings and a Buy for both existing shareholders and new entrants.

Wrap up of Allete stock performance for 2025

Article February 7, 2025

Table 2 below shows estimated indicative returns for buyers of Allete stock around the time of my February 7, 2025 article:

Table 2

Table 2 shows there is an opportunity to exit currently and lock in a rate of return 8.3% per year for the duration of the investment from February 7, 2025 to now. Continuing to hold will likely slightly dilute the rate of return due to the longer duration at lower rates of return of 6% to 7% per Table 1 above. The question whether to hold or exit now will revolve around the attractiveness of a 6% to 7% rate of return compared to alternative investments available.

Article June 21, 2025

Table 3 below shows estimated indicative returns for buyers of Allete stock around the time of my June 21, 2025 article.

Table 3

Table 3 shows there is an opportunity to exit currently and lock in a rate of return 20.3% per year for the duration of the investment from June 20, 2025 to now. Continuing to hold will dilute the rate of return due to the longer duration at lower rates of return of 6% to 7% per Table 1 above. As discussed above, the question whether to hold or exit now will revolve around the attractiveness of a 6% to 7% rate of return for the balance period compared to alternative investments available.

Summary and conclusions

During the course of 2025, Allete stock has offered opportunities for attractive returns, with a fairly certain exit, subject to regulatory approvals, all of which have now been received, but with uncertainty to the length of time to exit. Time to exit has now been narrowed down to by end of 2025. On that basis, buying the stock at current price levels should provide a fairly certain annualized rate of return in the range of 6% to 7%. On that basis, the stock continues to be rated a Buy for those seeking a short term investment opportunity providing a reasonable rate of return with relatively low risk.

data.title: Tesla Q3: The Storm Investors Don't See
data.description:
On October 22, 2025, Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) reported its Q3 ‘25 earnings. At first glance, the figures looked pretty decent. Revenue came in at ~$28 billion, outpacing market estimates of $26.7 billion by about 5%.

However, many, including myself, argue that this revenue beat was expected amid the EV tax credit still being available during Q3 ‘25. I believe consumers likely rushed to buy Teslas before the $7,500 federal tax credit expired in September, and as a result, boosted sales, which showed up in this quarter.

Earnings-wise, non-GAAP diluted EPS came in at $0.50, which missed market estimates of $0.56. That’s a notable miss on profitability, and this tells investors something critical about Tesla’s actual business health underneath the surface-level figures.

Then there’s the awkward sidenote about Tesla CEO Elon Musk asking for a $1 trillion compensation package - ostensibly to lock him in for the next decade or so as CEO by setting lofty milestones - as insurance he will have at least a "strong influence" over the robot army that the company is planning to build.

I’m not convinced investors are happy with how things are going.

With that, and for all the reasons outlined below, my Strong Sell rating for the company stays in place.

Q3 ‘25 Core Financials

According to its Q3 ‘25 financials, Tesla’s revenue rose 12% to ~$28 billion compared to the same period last year. The growth is driven by the overall revenue increases across all their business segments. Here’s a glance at Tela’s segmental revenue performance:

Segments

Q3 ‘24

Q3 ‘25

Change (%)

Automotive

$20,016

$21,205

5.9%

Energy Generation & Storage

$2,376

$3,415

43.7%

Services and Other

$2,790

$3,475

24.5%

Total

$25,182

$28,095

11.5%

Note: USD in Millions.

On the production side, Tesla manufactured a total of 447,450 vehicles, which was actually 5% lower compared to last year’s period. However, deliveries rose 7% to 497,099 vehicles. This obviously means Tesla delivered more than they produced, but this could also mean Tesla is drawing down inventory to meet demand.

The optimists might see that as positive, but personally, I think it’s an indicator that Tesla was aggressively pushing products out the door ahead of tax credit expiration. The numbers might look good now, but might not be sustained when artificial urgency subsides.

Tesla’s profitability suffered despite significant top-line improvements. Gross profit remained relatively flat at approximately $5 billion, despite notable revenue growth. Operating income declined about 40% YOY to $1.62 billion, which resulted in a compressed operating margin of just ~5.8%, about 500 basis points lower compared to the same period last year.

In perspective, a margin of 5.8% is quite respectable in the automotive landscape, but it’s pretty well below average in the tech industry - meaning the argument that Tesla is a tech leader, not an automotive player, doesn’t make sense.

If anything, Tesla’s compressed margin makes it worse. It also indicates erosion in Tesla's ability to convert revenue into profits.

Speaking of which, GAAP net income declined ~37% to $1.37 billion, $0.39 per share - significantly lower than the $0.62 in the prior year period.

However, there were some bright spots. For example, free cash flow increased 46% to ~$4 billion, and Tesla's total cash, cash equivalents, and investments rose ~24% to $41.65 billion. Despite the notable decline in profitability, Tesla still maintains a solid balance sheet. It’s good news for now, but cash generation in a single quarter cannot offset the fundamental issues from its core automotive business for long.

TSLA Stock Profile & Performance

All that financial movement has to reflect on TSLA stock, right? So let’s see how the stock’s been performing.

At the time of this publication, TSLA stock trades at ~$439 per share. The stock gained 8.70% since the beginning of the year and 101% over the past year. Notably, it has increased 214% over the past five years.

Yes, those are impressive returns by any measure, but what concerns many investors like me is that these gains have been largely driven by hype and speculation about Tesla’s vision, particularly in AI and robotics - not its core automotive business, nor by real improvements in business fundamentals.

The proof is in the valuation. At around $439 per share, Tesla trades at a FWD P/E (GAAP) ratio of about 314x - that’s about 1,519% higher than the sector average. In perspective, traditional automotive companies like Ford (F) and General Motors (GM) trade at 16.58x and 7.76x, respectively, well below the sector average of 19.40x.

Now, many people are saying that Tesla is a tech company, so it shouldn’t be surprising that it’s trading at sky-high valuations. But the argument crumbles following a quick look at its “peers.”

To be sure, the tech sector’s median FWD P/E is 33.14. Nvidia (NVDA), the undisputed leader in AI, trades at 41x, approximately 24% higher than the median.

So, even high-growth tech companies rarely command a premium this extreme. When you’re paying $314 for every $1 of annualized earnings, you’re essentially betting that the company will dramatically outperform all expectations for years to come. That might be possible for Nvidia, considering that AI enterprise adoption is still in its initial stages and the development of AI is ongoing. But for Tesla? By the looks of things, that remains a distant dream.

Personally, I think Tesla’s valuation is fraught with risks. Of course, when a stock trades at such a high level relative to its sector peers and its own earnings potential, a painful reality check is in order. Given the headwinds I’ll cover in the following sections, I also believe that the current valuation presents a poor risk-reward ratio for new investors at these levels.

What Could Happen In Q4 ‘25?

There’s several concerns that I think will notably impact the company’s performance, and honestly, I think they all warrant investors’ attention. Starting with the most immediate one:

The Demand Following The Expired Tax Credit

Tesla hit a record delivery of 497,009 vehicles in Q3 ‘25. However, I believe this is heavily boosted by customers rushing to buy before the $7,500 federal EV tax credit expired at the end of September.

In industry terms, this creates what's called a pull-forward effect. Those who would’ve normally purchased a Tesla in Q4 ‘25 or in the following quarters bought it early in Q3 ‘25 to not miss the tax benefit. That’s essentially borrowed demand from the future, which creates a concerning setup for the subsequent quarters as the artificial demand dissipates.

To make things worse, Tesla drew down its vehicle inventory during Q3 ‘25. Yes, they produced 447,450 vehicles, but delivered many more. Approximately 50,000 cars were sourced from existing inventory. As we move into Q4 ‘25, Tesla will need to replenish those inventory levels while also dealing with reduced customer urgency, now that the tax credit incentive is no longer in effect.

This is a perfect storm. Tesla will either need to cut prices significantly to maintain sales volume, which will erode margins even further, or it’ll have to accept lower delivery numbers. Neither option favors profitability.

Now, Tesla is trying to mitigate the impact by introducing a cheaper version of their main models. They released the Model 3 Standard and Model Y Standard in early October 2025. These are priced at $36,990 and $39,990, respectively. These are essentially price cuts of approximately $5,500 for the Model 3 and $5,000 for the Model Y premium versions.

It sounds like a good strategy at first, but the new Model Y is actually ~$2,500 more expensive than what you could have paid just weeks earlier with the tax credits. Meanwhile, the Model 3 Premium with tax credit was ~$34,990. The new standard model without the credits is $36,990.

Not to mention that, to achieve those lower prices, Tesla also stripped out a ton of features, making the standard models considerably less appealing. So, despite introducing supposedly more affordable models, Tesla vehicles are that much more expensive overnight.

That said, we saw the same pattern with Tesla’s peers. Most of them reported strong Q3 ‘25 EV sales, and also benefited from the tax credit rush.

For this reason, investors like myself are asking the same question: how far will sales fall after the subsidy is gone?

Regulatory Credit Environment

This next concern is huge, and surprisingly, not many are paying attention to it.

In Q3 ‘25, revenue from automotive regulatory credits fell 44% YOY to $417 million from $739 million in the same period last year. Tesla generates these credits at near-zero marginal cost simply by producing EVs. So that 44% decline represents a discrepancy of ~$322 million in what is supposedly a pure profit.

But, you might be asking, what exactly are these credits, and why are they so important? These regulatory credits are a mechanism used by the government to incentivize the production of zero-emission vehicles, which are primarily EVs. Under programs like EPA’s emissions standard and California’s Zero Emission Vehicle mandate, automotive companies are required to meet a specific fleet-wide emission target, which varies per regulation.

The point is that companies that produce EVs, like Tesla, generate surplus credits. And traditional automakers that still predominantly sell gas-powered vehicles can buy these credits from Tesla to help meet their regulatory obligations and, of course, avoid paying fines. For most automakers, buying credits is cheaper than paying the fines, which is why this is happening.

For Tesla, tax credits have translated to a significant revenue stream of nearly free money. According to industry reports, Tesla has generated ~$10.6 billion from selling these credits since 2019. And here’s a funny but nevertheless concerning fact: In Q1 ‘25, Tesla’s regulatory credit sales reached $595 million, which actually exceeded their bottom line figure of $409 million for that quarter.

So it might not be unfair to say that these credits have been masking the weakness of Tesla’s core automotive business, as, without them, Tesla would have lost money in that quarter.

And then there’s another kick in the shin: the Trump administration and Republican-led Congress passed budget legislation that effectively eliminates the financial penalties for automakers that fail to meet zero-emission standards.

No penalties means no fines, and if there are no fines, traditional automakers have no reason to buy credits from Tesla. And with sheer irony, CEO Elon Musk himself pushed for eliminating all automotive subsidies, stating that it would only help Tesla by putting everyone on a level playing field.

And with that, the company has effectively lost two of its most significant financial advantages: EV tax benefits and regulatory credit sales.

The Vote on the $1 Trillion Compensation Package

And then, we have the proposed $1 trillion compensation plan for Musk, which will be put to a vote on November 6, 2025.

The compensation plan is broken down into twelve tranches that are contingent upon the company reaching several market cap and business goals. For example, a few of the requirements are growing the market cap by trillions, 20 million cumulative vehicle deliveries, and delivering one million Optimus robots. If all the goals are realized, the entire package is expected to be fully vested in ten years.

Unsurprisingly, Musk has described the plan in dramatic terms, framing it as a way to ensure he can control a “robot army” once these milestones are achieved.

And again, to no one’s surprise, the plan is meeting heavy backlash. This compensation package would bring Musk’s Tesla ownership from the low tens to over 20%, which would give him unprecedented control over the company. It could overshadow the board and other shareholders, giving him near-unchecked authority over massive, unproven technologies. I’ve read enough science fiction to be wary of anything involving control of a million robots, thank you very much.

Beyond that, the size and structure of the award raise corporate governance questions, making some analysts fear what stunts Musk would pull to reach the high goals.

At the end of the day, investor trust is earned, and even the hint of overreach might negatively affect market confidence in the company.

Verdict

All things being fair, Tesla has some positive aspects going for it. The company’s balance sheet is quite strong. Free cash flow was ~$4 billion in Q3 ‘25, which is a solid figure. The company continues to lead the U.S. EV market and has achieved significant improvements across its business segments.

However, I believe the company is currently facing more headwinds than tailwinds. Elon Musk often talks big about his company’s vision but says very little about timelines and actual figures. In fact, management provided investors with almost no concrete guidance about what to expect moving forward, except for vague comments such as “sufficient liquidity to fund our product roadmap” and “we expect our hardware-related profits to be accompanied by an acceleration of AI.” Unfortunately, this has been Tesla’s pattern for years.

At ~$439 per share and a FWD P/E ratio of 314x, TSLA stock is priced as if all those problems are already solved, and that the company is already executing perfectly on its vision around AI and robotics. To me, that’s a massive bet with poor odds, given what I’m actually seeing right now.

My Strong Sell rating remains unchanged.

data.title: VLO Update: Crack Spreads Support Earnings For The Refiner
data.description:
I last wrote about Valero Energy Corp (NYSE:VLO) on Seeking Alpha before the November 2024 U.S. election. On Sept. 3, 2024, I concluded with the following:

We could see lots of volatility in the refining company’s shares over the coming weeks due to uncertainty caused by the U.S. election and U.S. energy and tax policy for the coming years. The bottom line is that the oil products processed by VLO will continue to power the U.S. and the world.

I also pointed out that:

Refiners like VLO do not speculate on the price of crude oil they purchase or gasoline, heating oil, jet, diesel fuels, or agricultural products that are inputs in biofuel production. However, the risk is in the refining spread for processing the raw materials into fuels. In petroleum, Crack spreads are a real-time indicator of refining profits or losses in the petroleum processing market. As crack spreads rise, profits tend to increase and vice versa.

VLO shares were trading at $141.35 on Sept. 3, 2024. The gasoline crack spread was at $12.76, with the distillate refining spread below $23 per barrel. Crack spreads are higher in late October 2025, and VLO shares were also higher, at over the $160 level, when the company reported its latest earnings on Thursday, Oct. 23. I maintain my "Buy" rating, as momentum and profitability remain robust.

Earnings expectations after a beat in all categories last quarter

As VLO prepared to release its latest earnings report on Oct. 23, the market expected higher EPS and lower revenues than the previous quarter.

As the chart shows, the market was expecting EPS above $3.00 per share, while VLO reported $2.28 in the prior quarter. Meanwhile, expectations for revenue of $29.25 billion were lower than the $29.89 billion reported on July 24, 2025, when Valero beat forecasts across all categories.

VLO continues to outperform expectations

On Oct. 23, before the U.S. stock market opened, VLO reported earnings of $3.66 per share and revenues of $32.17 billion. While exceeding the forecast, revenue decreased slightly from the $32.88 billion reported in the third quarter of 2024.

VLO returned $1.3 billion to shareholders through dividends and stock buybacks. Oil refining was the earnings driver, while record ethanol production added to earnings.

Crack spreads are a real-time indicator of refining earnings

The 3-2-1 crack spread is the theoretical margin calculated by subtracting the cost of three barrels of crude oil from the combined price of two barrels of gasoline and one barrel of distillate fuel. The crack spread measures the refining margin for processing a barrel of petroleum into oil products, drives refinery earnings, and is the critical factor for VLO’s quarterly results and the path of least resistance for the company’s shares.

The one-year chart of the 3-2-1 Crack Spread shows bullish price action in refining margins, which led to VLO’s latest earnings that beat consensus EPS and revenue forecasts.

Meanwhile, individual gasoline and distillate refining margins have moved higher since September 2024.

The chart of the gasoline crack spread shows it was around $17.25 per barrel on Oct. 23, higher than the $11.24 to $14.60 range in September 2024

The distillate refining spread, which ranged from $17.45 to $22.90 per barrel in September 2024, was substantially higher at $36.75 per barrel on Oct. 23, 2025.

Higher refining margins translate into increased profitability for companies that process crude oil into oil products, and VLO is a leading U.S. petroleum refining company.

Technical resistance is at the high from April 2024

VLO shares moved above the $170 level on Oct. 23 after the earnings beat and could be on a path to challenge the April 2024 record peak.

The chart shows that VLO’s technical target is at the April 2024 record high of $184.79 per share. At over $170, the stock is within striking distance of its all-time peak. At the $170 level, VLO had a market cap of over $50.3 billion. The highly liquid stock trades an average of 2.245 million shares per day. VLO pays shareholders a $4.52 dividend, yielding 2.66%.

VLO has been in a bullish trend since trading to a low of $31 per share in March 2020, when the global pandemic sent stocks and crude oil prices to lows. Since then, pullbacks in VLO shares have been buying opportunities

Seeking Alpha Factor Grades- Caution on valuation, clear sailing in other categories

Seeking Alpha’s latest Factor Grades for VLO are as follows:

VLO receives an A grade in revisions and an A- in momentum. The momentum grade will likely be upgraded after the latest price action following the earnings release. Profitability at a B is likely understated, while growth at C- is justified given the risk in oil and refining margins. The D- in valuation is a reason for caution, as Seeking Alpha’s valuation metrics for VLO highlighted an expensive price-to-book ratio and a high P/E ratio before the latest earnings. Keep an eye on if these metrics improve after considering the latest report.

VLO has been in a bullish trend since the March 2020 low, and all signs point to a continuation of that trend. Crack spreads are a real-time indicator for VLO and other oil refiners, and they remain at levels that support higher profits and new all-time highs for VLO shares.

data.title: Beyond Mergers: A Diversified Approach To Event-Driven Investment
data.description:
By Vikas Kapoor & Jorge Chang

For event-driven strategies, tie-ups and takeovers aren’t the only events that matter.

Event-driven investing seeks to extract alpha by capitalizing on price anomalies in shares of companies that are undergoing or affected by a corporate, investor or liquidity event. It’s one of the oldest and most well-researched hedge-fund strategies - the HFRI Event Driven Index dates to 1990. Over that period, it has delivered strong annualized risk-adjusted returns (Display) with just seven “down” years since inception.

Event-Driven: Steady Growth, Strong Risk-Adjusted Returns

Past performance does not guarantee future results.The risk-free rate used in calculating the historical Sharpe ratio is the FTSE Treasury Bill 1 Mon USD Index. Performance data for the HFRI Event-Driven Index reflects the cumulative since inception performance from January 1990 through April 2025.Source: eVestment, HFR and AllianceBernstein (AB)

But some event-driven strategies have biases that may make them less effective. In many cases, we think it’s from leaning too heavily into one type of event: merger arbitrage, where managers seek to extract risk premia associated with merger transactions over a defined outcome and timeline. These biases are visible at the index level. Investors might be surprised to learn that the HFRI Event Driven Index had a high correlation of roughly 0.9 to the HFRI Merger Arbitrage index between February 2020 and August 2025.

Make no mistake: we consider the recent acceleration of corporate mergers and acquisitions (M&A) activity a welcome sign of renewed dealmaking after years of muted activity. But for strategies that seek to capitalize on event-driven price anomalies, tie-ups and takeovers aren’t the only events that matter.

Hidden Equity Correlation in Merger Arbitrage

At first glance, leaning heavily into merger arbitrage substrategies may seem prudent. Over the long run, merger arbitrage has generated high-quality returns - roughly defined as returns that are consistent, understandable and uncorrelated over time to the broad equity market. Of the 42 HFRI hedge-fund indices, merger arbitrage has the seventh-smallest maximum drawdown and has logged just two down years over the past 35 years.

But the strategy can be inherently cyclical, driven by the ups and downs of deal flow, which can create challenges. And while merger arbitrage strategies tend to have little correlation to traditional markets, they can exhibit higher sensitivity during periods of market stress.

For event-driven strategies heavily focused on merger announcements - so-called “hard” catalysts with a defined outcome and timeline - this can result in a “long” bias to equity markets. In our view, this may increase the risk of losses when the market struggles.

Embracing a More Systematic Approach

But mergers aren’t the only catalysts that managers can seek out. There are also systematic catalysts - sometimes referred to as “soft” or “diversified” catalysts. They’re often events initiated by corporations, regulatory agencies or constrained investors such as passive fund managers. Unlike mergers, these events typically have a less defined effect on security prices and timeline for monetization.

Many event-driven investors look to these types of catalysts to seek out diversified returns with little or no correlation to the broad equity market. This may also offset some of the “point-in-time” beta to equity markets that merger arbitrage can introduce into a broader event-driven strategy. The sheer diversity of event types, however, creates challenges for fundamental event-driven managers. In the corporate segment alone, there are dozens of systematic catalyst categories that can be applied globally.

A Closer Look at Systematic Catalysts

The multitude of systematic catalysts can be organized into two subcategories: those that are sentiment-driven and those that are more diversified.

Sentiment catalysts create opportunities to capitalize on directional moves caused by discrete events. For example, an investor might interpret a company’s plans to buy back shares as an expression of confidence in its forward earnings. Or the anticipated rebalancing of a widely followed equity index might provide an opportunity to capture stock price momentum, as passive investors buy or sell shares.

Diversification catalysts focus on persistent market themes. An analyst’s rating change on a particular security, which may create an opening to take a long or short position, fits into this category.

With dozens of event-driven strategies and hundreds of substrategy event categories, we believe a rules-based, systematic approach is an essential ingredient to capture the returns associated with these catalysts. It enables investors to leverage hundreds of quantitative inputs to inform investment decisions and may prevent portfolios from becoming too concentrated in a smaller number of catalysts.

The alternative - conducting fundamental research and analysis on each and every transaction - would require resources that few investors have at their fingertips today.

It’s also important, in our view, to focus on opportunities to profit from catalysts via short positions. Many event-driven strategies tend to have a long bias, which we believe limits the opportunities to generate alpha. Adding short positions in event-driven strategies may also help keep them uncorrelated to the broader market in periods of stress.

Advances in machine learning are also enhancing the appeal of systematic strategies, allowing for a quantitative and AI-driven decision-making process that can interpret vast data sets and help identify and incorporate a wider range of catalysts with the potential to affect prices.

None of this suggests M&A activity won’t continue to anchor event-driven investing. But we think a broader and more diverse event-driven approach with the ability to go long and short has the potential to reduce correlations and make portfolios more durable across market environments.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Broadcom: OpenAI Shocker Factored In Already
data.description:
In a somewhat surprising move, Broadcom Inc. (NASDAQ:AVGO) signed a blockbuster deal with OpenAI (OPENAI). The custom chip company had already been speculated to have a large deal with the generative AI leader, so the size and scale were very unexpected. My investment thesis is more neutral on the stock, with the scale of the AI business still a relative issue.

OpenAI Spending Spree

Broadcom announced a surprise mega partnership with OpenAI to produce custom XPUs and networking equipment for AI data centers. The companies announced plans to procure enough custom AI accelerators for 10 GW of power.

The news was shocking, as the market generally assumed a new $10 billion custom chip customer announced with FQ3'25 earnings was OpenAI. In addition, OpenAI just announced a 6 GW GPU deal with Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), questioning the capacity to even finance another major chip order.

Either way, the OpenAI deal added $100+ billion to the AI chip backlog, but the stock didn't soar. Broadcom had previously forecast a $60 to $90 billion AI opportunity with 50% to 60% growth rates, and this OpenAI deal likely boosts the SAM.

The deal starts in H2 '26 (similar to with AMD) and is forecast to be completed by the end of 2029. In essence, the deal lasts no more than 3.5 years to deploy 10 GWs of AI accelerator racks, with OpenAI designing the chips and Broadcom producing the chips and throwing in key networking gear.

The big question is the revenue value from such a massive AI chip deal. Nvidia (NVDA) had placed a $35+ billion chip value on 1 GW of power, but OpenAI is developing internal chips to lower the cost. Broadcom should produce far less revenue per GW, but the estimates wildly vacillate with the company providing both racks of custom AI accelerators and ethernet networking connections.

The Futurum Group appears to place the per GW value for Broadcom chips at $28 billion per this tweet from CEO Daniel Newman, which would make the deal worth upward of $280 billion. These XPU prices wouldn't appear to offer the discounted costs and lower token prices for inference to warrant the major development work.

The Next Platform forecasts the custom XPUs and networking equipment to amount to a price below the AMD costs at somewhere below $20 billion per GW as follows:

OpenAI is motivated to get it done for enlightened self-interest reasons. But even then, this will end up being a hell of a lot more money for Broadcom than that $10 billion coming from Mystery Customer Number 4. Like somewhere around $160 billion to $200 billion over four years inclusive from Actual Customer Number 5, OpenAI.

Broadcom just released the Thor Ultra networking chip to connect hundreds of thousands of XPUs to drive large AI workloads. Presumably, OpenAI will use this AI Ethernet Interface Card as part of the deal, or newer versions as the data centers roll out in future years.

Considering the quick timeframe and 10 GW of power, Broadcom would add somewhere around $40 to $50 billion in annual revenues from this deal. The unknown is how much of this OpenAI deal factored into the company's prior projection for the AI market opportunity of $60 to $90 billion.

Broadcom ended FQ3 with $5.2 billion in AI revenues, with a forecast for FQ4 revenues of $6.2 billion. The company signed a $10 billion deal in the last quarter, now assumed to be xAI, so the business is running at a $25 billion annual rate in the October quarter.

The OpenAI deal gets Broadcom to the targeted AI revenue range in FY27, with limited revenues from this deal in FY26. The big question becomes whether OpenAI can even afford all of these chip deals.

The genAI company has impressively boosted revenues to over $1 billion per month, but OpenAI is losing billions now. The company lists 800 million weekly active users, with the company forecasting sales soaring to $125 billion by 2029 via $50 billion in subscription revenues and the rest from software and programming tools.

A lot has to go right to reach this enormous revenue target in just 5 years. Not to mention, the company has already announced 26 GW worth of chip deals, and the power issues could become a big problem.

Flat Stock

The stock hasn't rallied since the deal was announced prior to the market open on October 13. Broadcom had traded as high as $350 in the prior week and now trades at just $337.

As a prime example of why the stock hasn't moved, the consensus analyst estimates haven't dramatically hiked the future revenue targets. The consensus estimates for FY27 revenues have only jumped by ~$4 billion to $109 billion.

The numbers were already boosted dramatically following the big FQ3 earnings beat and AI revenue guided up for FQ4. A big part of the boost was the $10 billion new AI XPU deal with a hyperscaler.

Broadcom is forecast to generate FY25 total revenues in the $63 billion range, and the new FY27 target already adds $46 billion in sales during the 2-year period. The big unknown is whether this fully incorporates the revenue potential of OpenAI, and the numbers would suggest bigger upside, though the analyst estimates at $135 billion for FY28, possibly incorporate a longer ramp-up period into estimates.

The stock trades at just 28x FY27 EPS estimates of $12.14. Though, Broadcom isn't crazy expensive, the company has to grow EPS by 80% during this period for an investor to only get the stock at 28x these future earnings.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that our view is more neutral on Broadcom, with the stock not rallying on the massive OpenAI deal. The company has to produce massive growth over the next few years to just reach a more reasonable multiple valuation, though acknowledging Broadcom might actually beat those targets. Either way, the shocking OpenAI deal already appears factored into most of the financial targets of Broadcom prior to the deal announcement.

data.title: O-I Glass Is One Of The Strongest Prospects On The Market
data.description:
One of the absolute best investment opportunities on the market today is O-I Glass (NYSE:OI), in my opinion. This business is one that I've written about favorably in the past. My last article about it, published in early July of this year, discussed a major transformation that the enterprise is making. Management is aiming to significantly boost profitability over the next few years. Even if we ignore the prospect of its trading multiples expanding as it improves, the company seems to offer significant upside potential. That is exactly why I reaffirmed it in that article as a "Strong Buy" candidate. Since then, investors have been given a gift in the form of a major decline in the firm's share price. The stock is down 25.4% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 5.9%. Though to be fair, since I upgraded it to a "Strong Buy" in January of this year, the stock is up 9.8%, narrowly beating out the 9.4% rise the S&P 500 experienced.

Earnings are around the corner, with management expecting to announce results for the third quarter of 2025 after the market closes on Nov. 4. They currently anticipate a decline in revenue. Profitability, meanwhile, is expected to improve markedly. With management continuing to focus on cost-cutting initiatives, and the stock trading at dirt-cheap multiples, this is certainly one of the most appealing candidates out there. Although I do not currently own shares in the business, I am getting increasingly more interested in picking up some.

This transformation has legs

The most recent financial data that we have regarding O-I Glass does not really paint all that great a picture. Take the second quarter revenue as an example. During that period, management reported sales of $1.71 billion. That's down 1.3% compared to the $1.73 billion the company reported a year earlier. This drop in revenue occurred entirely because of weakness in Europe, with revenue plunging 7.6% from $802 million to $741 million. Management’s explanation for this is that lower selling prices on its goods negatively affected revenue by $41 million. The company also saw a 9% drop in glass container shipments thanks to lower shipments for beer, wine, and spirits customers. This impacted revenue negatively by $76 million. Things would have been even worse had it not been for a $56 million benefit that the company got because of foreign currency fluctuations as the euro strengthened relative to the U.S. dollar.

Looking at the data deeper, management seems to think that the weakness in Europe was a short-term issue. They specifically mentioned a supplier-related delay at a major plant reconfiguration project. This plant is now ramping up production, which means that there should be a return to more normal levels of sales. There seems to have been other issues as well. For instance, management mentioned that the first quarter of this year saw a disproportionately large amount of shipments in beer and wine, with that attributable to trade policy uncertainty. So in essence, it seems as though customers were, to an extent, front-loading demand. And that has now come back to bite us. Other issues like broader economic uncertainty and unfavorable weather conditions also played a role.

This drop in revenue was offset in large part by a jump in sales from the Americas. Overall revenue here at home grew 4.9% from $899 million to $943 million. The company benefited during this time from higher selling prices that pushed sales up by $22 million. But unfortunately, a lot of this seems to have been in response to cost inflation that the company pushed onto its customers. Even with that, however, glass container shipments grew 4%, which pushed sales up $50 million year over year. Shipments for beer and spirits customers were particularly positive. But as certain central and South American countries saw their currencies weaken compared to the U.S. dollar, foreign currency fluctuations hit the business to the tune of $28 million.

As revenue for the business dropped, profitability declined. Overall net income went from $57 million to negative $5 million. This was even though segment profits for the Americas jumped from $106 million to $135 million, certainly because higher shipments pushed profitability up by $10 million while the increases in prices were great enough to allow an extra $7 million improvement on the bottom line. Problematic, however, was the part of the company dedicated to its operations in Europe. Segment profits dropped from $127 million to $90 million. Lower selling prices did a lot of the damage here, pushing profitability down by $24 million. And lower shipments pushed profitability down another $18 million.

It is important to note this was not the only impact. The bigger problem for the company ended up being restructuring costs, asset impairment charges, and other related expenses, which totaled $113 million in the second quarter of this year. This is all part of the company's plan to restructure its operations to boost profitability in the long run. Adjusted net profits actually improved nicely, growing from $69 million to $82 million. Other profitability metrics were largely negative, with operating cash flow plummeting from $250 million to $155 million, and EBITDA declining from $324 million to $317 million. However, if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase in operating cash flow from $202 million to $226 million.

The chart above shows financial performance for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time last year. Revenue, profits, and cash flow metrics, were largely down during this time. And in the charts below, you can see the contributions to these changes (from a revenue perspective), with the Americas once again proving to be an area of strength for the company while Europe proved problematic.

Ignore the pain

At first glance, you may wonder why I would be so bullish about a company experiencing weakness across most major categories. This is especially true when you consider that this year is likely to be worse than last year was. If we continue to follow the path that we have been on, we would expect adjusted operating cash flow to fall from $614 million last year to $562 million this year, and for EBITDA to decline from $1.10 billion to $1.05 billion. However, the great news is that management is carefully executing a plan aimed at boosting returns for shareholders in the long run. In my article about the business published in July, I discussed how management has been focused on significantly reducing costs and optimizing its assets through its Fit to Win program.

This initiative has already started to bear fruit. Management claimed that they captured $84 million in annual run rate synergies in the second quarter of this year, bringing total savings for the first half of 2025 up to $145 million. This year in its entirety should see cost reductions exceed the $250 million target that management set. And by 2027, the company expects to grow total savings to $650 million. This is a multi-stage process, with the first phase expected to be completed by the middle of next year. One such example of these cost savings initiatives occurred in late July of this year when the company finalized its plans for the indefinite suspension of one of its furnaces and the closure of one of its plants here in the Americas. But as I mentioned, these kinds of changes don't come cheap. For the third quarter of this year, for instance, management expects $45 million in expenses associated with closures.

Starting next year, the company will be focused on profitable growth, while 2028 and beyond offer the opportunity to grow using joint ventures, partnerships, and mergers and acquisitions. When it comes to the changes the company is making next, one of the things at the top of its list is the continued rollout of its organization effectiveness program across 15 different facilities. This followed a successful pilot at one of its plants. If everything does go according to plan, then by 2027, the company expects to generate EBITDA of around $1.45 billion, with that metric expanding to $1.65 billion by 2029.

The chart above shows how the company is valued using these estimates and comparable estimates for adjusted operating cash flow. Even if we use figures from today, the stock is incredibly cheap. And in the table below, you can see how it stacks up against five other similar enterprises. All five of these are meaningfully cheaper than it is, which signals to me that even keeping operations stable should result in upside for shareholders.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
O-I Glass 3.1 6.1
Ardagh Metal Packaging SA (AMBP) 4.8 8.4
TriMas (TRS) 16.8 14.9
Myers Industries (MYE) 7.2 8.4
Greif (GEF) 7.0 8.6
Silgan Holdings (SLGN) 13.3 10.0

In the chart below, I decided to see what kind of total upside the company would experience if it were to trade at the lowest of the multiples of these five competitors. I also looked at potential upside under the assumption that it were to trade at the average of them. The worst-case scenario among these calculations was an upside of 54.8%. Especially in this market, that is massive. And in the other scenarios, we would be looking at an upside of between 136.6% and 235.6%.

There is another way to look at the picture as well. I firmly believe that the spread between how O-I Glass is valued and how other comparable firms are valued is irrational. But even if we ignore the firm narrowing this gap, and focus only on future prospects, upside potential is significant. In the chart below, you can see how much annualized upside and total upside the company would experience if it were to trade at the same multiples it does right now while achieving the targets that management has set for it. These are significant increases that could be captured.

Bringing it back to the present

After the market closes on Nov. 4, management will be reporting results for the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. They currently anticipate revenue of $1.66 billion. That would be down from the $1.68 billion the company reported the previous year. The table below, however, shows that both earnings per share and adjusted earnings per share are forecasted to improve. And while there is no guarantee, I would bet that if those improve, the cash flows will also get better. But of course, investors should keep a close eye on this and change their outlook for the company as data dictates is necessary.

Takeaway

Currently, I firmly believe that O-I Glass is a stellar prospect. The company is making major changes aimed at increasing shareholder value. And I am all for it. This is a long-term play, and I am getting increasingly closer to participating in it from a shareholder perspective. Naturally, this would lead me to rate the company a "Strong Buy" at this time.

data.title: Idaho Strategic Resources: A Solid Tracker Of The Historic Gold Price Rally
data.description:
Shares of American mining company, Idaho Strategic Resources, Inc. (NYSE:IDR), are up 106% (YoY), and have outperformed the S&P 500 index by more than 90% (YoY) and the Gold spot price by more than 45% (YoY).

In Q2 2025, IDR reported a revenue increase of 54.7% (YoY) and a surge of 39.34% (YoY) in H1 2025 as compared to the same period in the prior year. Despite trading at a fair valuation, I will explain why I am rating this stock as a hold, given the positive movement in gold prices and the expected bottom-line growth dynamics into 2026.

Company Earnings Overview

While IDR is engaged in the exploration, production, and sale of gold, silver, and rare earth elements (REE), gold is its main source of revenue. In its Q2 2025 earnings report, IDR stated that its 54.7% (YoY) growth in revenue was largely due to the 57.71% (YoY) increase in the price of spot gold that stood at an average of $3,223.38.

In Q2 2025, sales from gold accounted for approximately $9.6 million, with smelting and refining charges reducing the total sales to nearly $9.5 million. In the 6 months to June 30, 2025, the price of spot gold grew 45% (YoY) at $3,043.19, while IDR's revenues at the same period of time rose 39% (YoY). Interestingly, the price of spot gold is up 66.35% (YTD). In the near-term, the rally is supported by analysts' expectations of additional federal interest rate cuts, the recent US government shutdown, as well as an increasing demand for the commodity as a safe-haven, especially with the ongoing political tensions.

Further, IDR seems to be getting it right when it comes to the sale of silver concentrates. Sales of the commodity grew 61% (YoY) in the 3 months to June 30, 2026, and 38.6% (YoY) in the 6 months to June 30, 2025.

Adding to this, barely a year after the discovery of the Red Star Area in the Golden Chest Mine, IDR recently reported the discovery of "high-grade intercepts within the Red Star's broader mineralized zones." While making the announcement, IDR's CEO stated,

The Red Star area is a unique prospect at the Golden Chest compared to the typical veins we've mined and explored previously. Traditionally, at the Golden Chest, our operations have centered around narrow, high-grade veins within waste rock. The Red Star is unique in that there are broad zones of lower-grade mineralization surrounding the higher-grade intercepts. While there is more exploration work warranted from the newly developed underground drill stations, the Red Star discovery is exciting because the broader zones of mineralization could be amenable to higher productivity mining methods."

I believe IDR now stands to have effective drilling estimates for the underground drilling stations established at the Red Star's 941-drill levels. The company indicated that the broader mineralization zones were unique as compared to the other narrow high-grade veins located at the waste rock, with a similar discovery recently made by GoldMining. All this comes against the backdrop of IDR's addition to the VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ) as of September 2025. This ETF has net assets worth about $18.92 billion and is already up 96.36% (YoY). This addition means greater visibility of IDR, which is expected to grow investor interest into 2026.

Thoughts on Next Earnings Review

IDR is expected to release its Q3 2025 on November 3, 2025. I expect the management to discuss, among other things the recent purchase of the Toboggan project from Hecla Mining Company for a cash deal worth $300,000. The deal completed in August 2025, saw the company expand its holdings (with an additional 143.5 acres) at the "Murray gold belt region" where IDR also has the Golden Chest Mine. Over the past month, IDR has risen over 19% (MoM), marking 9 years since IDR began production at the Golden Chest Mine in 2016.

Additionally, IDR is expected to reap from a recent breakthrough in its fuel design made in collaboration with Clean Core Thorium Energy, dubbed Advanced Nuclear Energy for Enriched Life or ANEEL (an alternative nuclear energy resource). An initial technical assessment by Canada's Nuclear Waste Management Organization (NWMO) recently reported that it had found "no fundamental barriers to safe long-term waste disposal" of ANEEL. Apart from gold production, IDR has significant thorium concentrates, the largest in the US. This finding is essential in advancing the company's thorium production and possible future commercialization.

Valuation

I will use the price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio to evaluate IDR since the company recorded positive earnings in Q2 2025. IDR's net income increased 28.25% (YoY) in Q2 2025 to $2.77 million and 1.1% (YoY) in the 6 months to June 30, 2025, to $4.38 million. IDR's forward P/E ratio sits at 53.59X against a forward EPS of 0.75. To calculate the future price, I will multiply 53.59 by 0.75 to get $40.19, which is IDR's current trading price (as of this writing). This analysis means that IDR is trading at its fair valuation.

I will compare the performance of IDR with some of its peers using this valuation method. My analysis will use companies with considerable margin expansions (as seen in their year-to-date performances) and profit leverage.

Forward PE Forward EPS Future Price Current price Valuation difference YTD analysis
IDR 53.59X 0.75 $40.19 $40.19 0% (Trading at fair valuation) 294.41%
K92 Mining Inc. (OTCQX:KNTNF) 16.08X 0.87 $13.99 $13.95 +0.3% (YoY) trading at fair valuation 131.72%
SSR Mining Inc. (SSRM) 14.49X 1.68 $24.34 $22.66 +7.41% (YoY) slightly undervalued +223.28%
Alamos Gold Inc. (AGI) 24.55X 1.45 $35.60 $32.49 +9.6% (YoY) slightly undervalued 75.60%

As shown in the analysis above, both IDR and KNTNF are trading at fair valuation, while AGI and SSRM are slightly undervalued (as far as their PE ratios are concerned). IDR registered the highest upside on YTD analysis, followed by SSRM at +223% (YTD). As indicated in my analysis, the price of spot gold has already crossed the $4200 mark and may likely hit $4,500 if the gold rally continues into 2026.

Risk to the valuation

IDR's revenue increase, as earlier on indicated, was largely attributed to the gold price rally. A decline in the price of spot gold into 2026 will affect the company's revenue and have an adverse impact on the stock's price.

IDR recorded a 31% (YoY) increase in cost of sales in Q2 2025 at $4 million, while operating expenses also rose 202.6% (YoY) to $2.96 million. An increase in expenses in 2026 may likely cause IDR to record net losses. Overall expenses accounted for more than 73% of IDR's total revenues at about $7 million against the total sales of about $9.5 million. Such a scenario will affect IDR's PE ratio and adversely affect the stock's price heading into 2026.

IDR sells its gold flotation concentrate to only 1 broker, H&H Metals. In Q2 2025, it did not record any sales of its unrefined gold-silver concentrates (dore).

Having a single broker will affect IDR's future earning capacity, especially if the broker terminates the contract.

Bottom Line

I have rated Idaho Strategic Resources as a hold in view of its expected positive momentum in line with the gold price rally and its recent addition to the VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF. IDR also recorded positive silver earnings that will likely grow its revenues into 2026. Further, IDR is poised for robust drilling after establishing unique, broad mineralized zones in the Red Star Area of the Golden Chest Mine. However, the stock is trading at a fair valuation and is facing an increase in overall expenses that may affect earnings into 2026.

data.title: Tesla Remains The Purest Expression Of The Market's Appetite For Optionality
data.description:
I'd like you to imagine that you could invest in an early-stage startup that is working to build breakthrough technologies with the potential to redefine entire industries. This company isn't turning a profit yet and only generates a small amount of sales. However, if it does manage to succeed, it faces a total addressable market, or TAM, expansion that is orders of magnitude larger than its current size. In this case, you wouldn't value this startup on the basis of its current business size, or how its earnings profile currently looks, because it is the optionality that gives it most of its value.

Now take the same thought experiment, and apply it to a giant company that's generating billions of dollars in profits from its core business, while, and this is key, it is simultaneously building technologies that could transform a multitude of industries altogether. Again, in this situation, as I argue in this analysis, traditional valuation metrics may not capture its true value at all.

To me, this company is Tesla Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA), which I genuinely have now come to see as a very strong representation of how the market values technological optionality, especially with the tailwinds that the AI revolution brings.

Those who have followed me for a while may know very well of my almost stubborn bearish stance on Tesla, which I have turned around very recently, after deeply reassessing the company's potential:

In this analysis, following the company's recent Q3 earnings release, I argue that Tesla embodies a form of technological optionality at a scale that few others can match, which makes it highly valuable. I discuss how promising its strides on AI-driven autonomy, energy storage, and humanoid robots actually are.

However, despite the promising potential of these technologies, I point out that assigning value purely to optionality is a complex and risky approach, and as such, I choose a neutral Hold rating for the stock.

Q3 Earnings Update

During Q3, Tesla reported a quarterly revenue total of $28.1 billion, up almost 12% from $25.2 billion brought in on Q3 '24. This was driven by a 6% jump in the company's automotive division, which represents over 75% of the company's revenue base, and 44% and 25% jumps in the energy generation / storage and services divisions, respectively. Each of these "smaller divisions" represent close to 12.5% of Tesla's total revenue.

A main driver for this revenue surge was the higher delivery numbers the company brought in, particularly from the Model Y, which continues to dominate Tesla's global sales. North America and China led the growth, bringing in 29% and 33% YoY growth respectively, while the EMEA and APAC markets also showed meaningful improvements.

On the gross profit side, the company saw its third consecutive quarter of a sequential climb from 16.3% in Q1, 17.2% in Q2, and now 18% in Q3. This was achieved as higher production volumes enabled better absorption of fixed costs, while other supply chain optimizations helped reduce per-unit expenses. Additionally, Tesla's growing software and services business segment, which is brings in much higher margins, also contributed to the positive margin trend.

I'm also glad to observe a similar trend with the operating margin, which also saw its third consecutive quarter of a sequential climb, with 2.1% in Q1, 4.1% in Q2, and 5.8% this time in Q3. This, of course, highlights a growing degree of operational discipline, despite facing pressures from higher logistics costs and occasional component shortages.

Adjusted EBITDA, which was $2.8 billion in Q1 and $3.4 billion in Q2, rose to $4.2 billion in Q3. Similarly, net income for the quarter, which was $409 million in Q1 and $1.2 billion in Q2, climbed to $1.4 billion in Q3. This bottom line figure, which is $0.50 in EPS terms, fell short of the EPS expectation of $0.56

Overall, this quarter solidified the notion that Tesla is highly able to scale its operations efficiently while maintaining healthy profitability across multiple segments. The strong demand seen from the Model Y signals that Tesla is still very much a dominant player in the EV landscape, with the capacity to outpace its competitors.

The Valuation Angle

Before I reversed gear on my bearish position on Tesla, the number one argument I had against the company was its valuation. To many, a company bringing in TTM earnings of only $5.9 billion, trading at a market cap of almost $1.5 trillion is simply beyond reasonable. For the longest time, I, too, had agreed with that line of thinking. However, however, I now strongly believe that the company's valuation is actually a reflection of the sheer optionality that is supposedly embedded into its technological ecosystem.

This seems very clear from the latest earnings call, where CEO Elon Musk highlighted all the progress the company has been making in so many frontier domains, each of which hold immense transformative potential.

AI Driven Autonomy

The most exciting among these, in my view, is Tesla's work on AI-driven autonomy. Despite this domain facing a range of technical and regulatory hurdles, I see the trajectory as promising, and almost inevitable.

In fact, unlike a typical startup working on building the set up for intelligent autonomy from scratch, Tesla, actually faces a substantial advantage, which is its massive installed base of vehicles already equipped with the necessary sensors, cameras, and full self-drive ("FSD") computation hardware. As such, the company does not have to wait several years for new hardware deployments, but could simply upgrade millions of cars on the road simultaneously through a comprehensive software update, which would instantly scale the impact of any AI improvement. It also minimizes any incremental capex per unit, which improves the rate of return.

As a result, the company's installed base actually allows for monetization before full technological maturity is achieved. That's brilliant, in my opinion, because it represents the potential of an exponential unlock, where each Tesla car is a future Robotaxi node. Once FSD does, in fact, cross regulatory approval, revenue per vehicle could see a shift from $0 hardware margin to recurring SaaS margins. That would be a game changer for Tesla economics, and completely shift the dynamics of how we approach the valuation of TSLA stock.

Energy Storage

Similarly, the company has been making some very noticeable strides on the energy storage landscape with its Megapacks, which is Tesla's large-scale industrial energy storage system. It is designed to store clean energy, help stabilize the grid by delivering energy when demand spikes, and also prevent power outages. This, again, clearly is a strong point of interest, as emphasized by Musk in the latest earnings call.

In my view, Megapacks introduces an undeniable layer of optionality into Tesla, because it potentially opens up multiple revenue streams for the company by serving utilities, commercial projects, and large-scale renewable energy integrations. Tesla would essentially be earning through sales, installations, and software-driven grid services. In fact, with the smart software the company is currently developing, Tesla would actually be able to optimize energy storage for demand response, frequency regulation, and capacity markets.

Where this becomes truly strategic, however, is in terms of the energy demands of the AI era. As AI data centers consume unprecedented amounts of electricity, the limiting factor for AI growth is likely to push onto the energy fronts, both in terms of availability and reliability. In fact, without firm and dispatchable energy storage that can stabilize increasingly volatile grids, the AI economy could face serious hurdles in terms of scaling up. Remarkably, Tesla actually aims to achieve this through its Megapacks, powered by renewable sources.

These grid-scale energy assets designed to turn intermittent renewable power into a stable and predictable energy supply required for (always-on) AI compute. In fact, with the AI context, energy reliability is highly critical, as server downtime could be catastrophic to users, considering how widely permeating AI applications are expected to reach.

Megapacks, which are engineered to be modular and customizable, are, as the Tesla website puts it, "infinitely scalable." This means that as AI compute demand grows, data centers, along with utility projects, can actually continuously expand their energy storage capacity simply by adding more Megapack units to their setup. The fact that each unit integrates seamlessly with others, allows operators to scale from a single installation to multi-gigawatt systems without having to redesign the entire infrastructure layout.

Coupled with Tesla’s software, which optimizes performance across all connected units, this modularity ensures reliable and efficient energy supply, capable of meeting the always-on power needs of next-generation AI workloads. As an added bonus, it does so by also supporting renewable energy integration, in the process.

Humanoid Robots

Finally, we turn to what CEO Elon Musk has referred to as potentially "the biggest product of all time," which is of course, the Optimus line of humanoid robots. Unlike electric cars, which address a finite transportation need, Optimus is designed to tackle the entire labor problem, by offering the possibility of automating tasks across factories, offices, and even homes. According to Musk's visions, these humanoid bots, which, unlike specialized, industrial grade robotics, are not restricted to a single function, and are engineered to be 'trained' to perform virtually any tasks human beings can. As such, mundane and monotonous physical (and potentially even intelligence-based) tasks could eventually be supplemented by Optimus robots, which potentially frees up human labor for more creative, strategic, or interpersonal work.

Of course, this is not some futuristic fantasy, as Tesla has already deployed early versions of Optimus within its own factories on a trial basis. Although their numbers are limited, the robots are reported to be working autonomously, performing repetitive and labor-intensive tasks that were previously handled by humans. These applications include predictable, but physically demanding tasks such as moving components, assisting with assembly lines, and performing quality inspections.

I find this approach smart because, by the time Optimus reaches consumer and enterprise markets, it would essentially be operationally validated and "battle-tested" in high-volume, high-stakes environments.

In my view, many are overlooking the sheer level of new economic productivity Optimus as a technology could potentially unlock, with around the clock deployment, offering greater precision, consistency, and efficiency. This could bring about efficiency gains in various sectors such as manufacturing, logistics, healthcare, and services, with Tesla positioned as the key enabler to this transformation.

This optionality makes Tesla highly valuable, because it demonstrates that the company is building and backing a single technology that could be transformed into a multi-industry, high-leverage growth engine.

Risk to Thesis

Tesla, is trading at a forward PE [GAAP] ratio of 314, compared to the consumer discretionary sector median P/E ratio of 19x. This is quite the substantial discrepancy, at its face value. However, as I have argued in my analysis above, these multiples do not reflect the sheer technology and transformative optionality embedded within the frontier areas the company is working on.

That being said, however, I do concur that assigning value to a company purely based on what its technologies might achieve is not very simple, and to be frank, somewhat speculative at its core. While the technologies the company is working on really do have the potential to transform entire industries and sectors alike, propelled by the AI proliferation boom, it remains unclear as of yet, to predict exactly how and when these technologies will generate meaningful revenue streams or profits. The realization of these developments is also conditional upon regulatory approvals, successful technical execution, and optimistic assumptions about their widespread adoption.

As such, while the optionality is valuable to Tesla, as investors, it is crucial to treat it as potential upside rather than guaranteed value. For this reason, I am not recommending a Buy at current prices, but rather a Hold, with a close eye on the company’s progress in these frontier domains.

Investor Takeaway

I believe those who have already entered a long position on the Tesla, prior to it reaching its current price, have the most favorable risk-reward dynamic with the stock. This is because they have already captured much of the upside from the core EV business, while retaining exposure to the transformative optionality embedded in the frontier areas Tesla is working on.

For new investors, entering in on current prices, the risk-reward dynamic is a lot more compressed, for the reasons I have highlighted above. There may be a gain upon entering a long position now, but I personally see that as being too high risk to consider. As such, I am giving TSLA a neutral, Hold rating.

data.title: BWX Technologies: Too Early For SMR (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

In January, I covered BWX Technologies (NYSE:BWXT), concluding that it was a lower-risk option in the SMR/nuclear segment, given its stable defense business and a still reasonable valuation at the time. Fast forward, and the SMR theme has reached hype levels, and BWX has seen its share price nearly double. I revisited the investment case and concluded that a downgrade to Hold is warranted.

Operating Model

As discussed in greater detail in my first article, BWX is primarily a defense contractor specializing in building and maintaining nuclear reactors for the US Navy (submarines) with highly stable and predictable contracts, revenue, and cash flow. On the commercial side, most of its revenue has come from uranium fuel fabrication, maintenance, and related services, with the Canadian Nuclear fleet (CANDU) its main customer. The launch and restart of traditional nuclear power plants in the US is also a source of added revenue, but what has the market and company excited is the construction and commercialization of SMRs (Small Modular Reactors), where BWX can apply its submarine reactor and uranium fuel know-how. It's part of the Pele project, which is testing the concept by building a 1.5MW nuclear power plant for the defense department. The company acquired Kinectrics for US$525m to increase its potential share in the SMR development. While I may fully agree that SMRs can be a substantial new growth avenue, the time to market is still years away and may be relevant in 2028 forward, while the fuel manufacturing could provide a new long-term revenue source, such as defense.

3Q25 Results

BWX should report a solid 3Q25 result with revenue trends accelerating on new contracts announced during the year, although the consensus expects a lower margin, possibly on revenue mix, more commercial vs defense, and cost inputs from recent raw material tariffs. The key data to look for is how the commercial business is evolving and how the recently closed Kinectrics acquisition is evolving.

Valuation

In the January valuation, I assigned a 2.1x PEG to BWX vs the consensus valuation of 2.4x PEG, which produced a US$153 price target for YE26 at 29x P/CE (cash earnings are net income, depreciation, and stock-based compensation). The stock has surfed the SMR wave as well as added around US$300m to forward revenue on new contracts, mostly in the defense segment. Thus, the performance is partially fundamentally based.

The current consensus price target for YE26 is US$203, which continues to be valued at 2.4x PEG, and I have raised my fair value PEG to the same level, which indicates the shares are fully valued to YE26, barring new contracts or M&A.

It's not likely that the SMR buildout impacts the company's earnings in a meaningful way until 2028, and the margin gains may be a challenge.

Peer Comps

I gathered consensus growth and valuation metrics for BWX peers that include defense and energy stocks. As can be seen, BWX trades more like a defense stock, as it should with a rich or premium valuation. In the energy area it's expensive vs GE Vernova (GEV) or Siemens Energy (SMEGF, SMNEY), which I have covered recently and rate with buy. However, BWX is a far more palatable idea vs the pure SMR stocks such as Oklo (OKLO) and NuScale (SMR). In my recent Okolo analysis, it's evident that the market is working on optimistic if not exaggerated views divorced from reality. A good compromise could be Cameco (CCJ), which encompasses the full nuclear supply chain.

Risk

Not a lot can go wrong as a primary defense contractor to the US nuclear submarine fleet. Perhaps the primary risk may be technological obsolescence, where someone invents a fission reactor. Other risks may be related to raw material supplies and inputs that come under tariffs or embargoes. The main risk to the share price is if the SMR development does not proceed at a brisk pace, as is expected.

Conclusion

I downgraded BWXT to hold from buy. While the stock’s valuation has incorporated some of the SMR hype, medium-term earnings are grounded and should support the shares as we wait for SMR technology to prove itself and be commercially deployed.

data.title: BUG: The Structure, Characteristics, And Mechanics Of This Cybersecurity ETF
data.description:
The Global X Cybersecurity ETF (NASDAQ:BUG) has been around since October 2019. With a 100% technology holding and a nearly 60% allocation to the Top 10 stocks, this is a highly concentrated play on the cybersecurity market. Concentration is even more when you look at the small portfolio of just 24 stocks. The ETF benchmarks itself against the Indxx Cybersecurity Index, which has very specific inclusion criteria, so this helps us understand how the fund's portfolio is structured.

The nuts and bolts of the ETF are pretty straightforward: an AUM of over $1 billion, an affordable ER of 0.51%, the Global X name behind the fund, nearly $10 million in daily ETF share liquidity, relatively healthy interest in options, strong long-term momentum, market-matching performance, and a turnover well under control for such a volatile grouping of securities.

On the flip side is the high tracking error against the index (it's getting better over time, though), the lack of a dividend, a Hold rating from Seeking Alpha's Quant algo, and some signs of weakening momentum in the near-term.

Now that you've seen a snapshot, let's dive in further and learn more about how this fund is structured, what drives it, and how it has performed over the long term.

How is the Global X Cybersecurity ETF Portfolio Assembled?

It's important to know that the ETF's portfolio is constrained by what the index includes and excludes. It has a hard limit of 40 securities, which further exacerbates the concentration risk. In order to be eligible for inclusion in the index, the security must satisfy several conditions:

  1. It should have a market cap of no less than $200 million, and in cases where the free float is less than 10% of TSO (total shares outstanding), the free float portion should be worth at least $1 billion. Once a company is included in the index, it needs to maintain a market cap of only $80 million, and its average daily trading volume (ADTV dollar average) should stay above $1.2 million. The initial criterion for eligibility is a 6M ADTV of $2 million.
  2. Recently IPO'ed companies are eligible, but must have a market cap that exceeds half of the index's constituents - above the mean, in other words; in such cases, the IPO could be as recent as 10 days prior to what the fund calls its "selection date". For smaller market cap IPOs, the timeline is set to 30 days prior.
  3. The listing of the security can either be in an established market or an emerging one, which allows the ETF to hold a wider global selection of cybersecurity notables.
  4. If there are multiple share classes, the most liquid one is chosen for inclusion.

After the selection process for the index is done with, there's the question of weighting. This also follows a set of rules that govern individual weightings.

  1. The default range of weighting is 0.3% to 6%, but each security needs to maintain an ADTV of at least 60% of the initial eligibility criterion of $2 million, which is $1.2 million.
  2. In the event that there are several +5% weighting allocations to a set of securities, their total weighting cannot exceed 40%. In such cases, the cap for the remaining constituents drops to 4.5%, and any remaining weighting after all these allocations are completed are distributed between the non-capped securities in proportion to their individual market capitalizations.
  3. The fund is rebalanced and reconstituted every six months on days called Effective Days, which come around on the second Friday of May and November.

Now that we know how the fund's portfolio is guided by its benchmark index, let's look at the actual holdings.

This is more of a moment-in-time situation because the fund has a surprisingly high annual turnover of nearly 25%, but you're not likely to see securities drop in and out of the Top 10 on a frequent basis due to the stringent selection, inclusion, and retention criteria set out by the index.

When it comes to geographic allocation, we see a majority of the fund's $1.1 billion AUM concentrated in U.S. securities - about 72% or so. That's a significant portion that adds more on top of what I mentioned earlier, and it's something to note because some of the largest players in cybersecurity are domestic U.S. companies. The Top 10 holdings for BUG do have a couple of Israeli and Canadian companies (some of whom also have headquarters in the U.S.), but the composition of the Top 10 is a pretty good mirror of the whole portfolio of 24 stocks.

The Cyclicality of Cybersecurity and the Mechanics of BUG's Performance

The cybersecurity industry is notoriously news-sensitive, and if I whittle it down to its core, incident-sensitive. There's a ton of evidence that the industry moves up through what's known as "reactive spending", implying elevated outlays for cybersecurity when major breaches feature in a given news cycle or a major development necessitates the need to pivot from traditional service models.

The best example of this would be the post-pandemic surge that I captured below in the Nasdaq CTA Cybersecurity Index (NQCYBR) chart.

That's a doubling of the index right there, between the initial pandemic scramble for work-from-home cybersecurity implementations right up to the peak in November of the following year.

Similarly, you'll see several of these cyclical, almost peristaltic upward movements of the index as a whole, and this essentially reflects revenue uptrends during these phases of the cycle.

This example below with revenue growth percentages for BUG's biggest holdings is for that one period between March 2020 and November 2021.

The next major development that's already been with us for nearly three years now is the AI landgrab. That's not a nice way to put it, I know, but that's exactly what it is - an attempt by the deepest corporate pockets in the world to capture the bulk of AI infrastructure so every enterprise is forced to subscribe to an endless stream of AI-driven products.

The positive takeaway for BUG is that there's now an almost inexhaustible availability of capital from both institutional and retail investors who have pushed the valuations of these companies to closely mirror their hyper-scaler counterparts that are actually building out that infrastructure. I give you BUG's Top 3 again.

So, that's what moves the cybersecurity industry right now, and I don't see that changing for the next ten years, at the very least. That means risk will remain high for investors in this sector, but the reward is likely to be just as high, if not higher.

As long as the current upcycle lasts, we're likely to see these revenue and valuation trends moving in lockstep. The good news for BUG is that it's managed to keep pace with the market despite this cyclical behavior.

Some Additional Considerations for BUG

An investment in this fund involves a lot of risk, for sure, and some keen investors might even say it's not worth the higher amount of risk just to be able to match the performance of the market. That's up to each individual to decide, but the other side of that argument is that it can be used tactically to capture strong momentum on every upcycle of the cybersecurity market.

Another consideration might be the lack of dividend, which means you only have price action on your side and there's opportunity cost at play when you hold the ETF on the downcycles. The balancing view is that there aren't any immediate tax implications if you hold BUG long-term.

Finally, keep in mind that the volatility of this ETF and its individual holdings also opens up a world of options. Literally. That means you can take advantage of both upcycles and downcycles to make money, regardless of which way the industry is moving. Selling cash-secured puts gives you the optionality of owning more BUG or more of its components at attractive prices when the cycle is headed down; conversely, buying long calls works well during upcycles, allowing you to capitalize on momentum while still keeping your costs down.

Irrespective of what you decide to do, know that BUG is one way to tap into an essential, almost commoditized but highly sticky niche within the grander realm of AI-related technologies.

This article answers these three main questions about BUG:

  • Are there concentration concerns with the BUG portfolio?
  • Does BUG come with higher risks and higher potential rewards than the average ETF?
  • Is BUG, and the cybersecurity industry as a whole, susceptible to cyclicality?

Editor's Note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.
